title: Story Navigation and Focus Mode
problem: Readers get lost in deep feature trees. Once you've drilled into a child story, there's no quick way to hop back up to the parent thread, collapse sibling noise, or isolate just the slice of the tree you care about. The app currently forces a linear scroll through everything — it has no concept of where you are or where you want to go.
analysis: Three distinct navigation gestures address three different reader postures. 'Hop up' is for the reader who followed a thread too deep and wants back to the parent context — a breadcrumb or back button that jumps one level up the lineage chain. 'Collapse thread' (from feat-2026-005) handles dismissal of sub-trees the reader actively doesn't want. 'Focus mode' is the most powerful: it isolates a single feature and its direct lineage (ancestors + children) as the only visible content, dimming or hiding everything else — like clearing the desk to work on one thing. Together they let readers navigate a feature tree the way a reader navigates a book: by chapter, not by scrolling the full manuscript.

decisions.decision: Focus mode dims non-focal cards in place rather than filtering the visible set to only focused + ancestors + children
decisions.rationale: Keeping all cards in the DOM preserves the spatial layout and lets the reader see the full tree shape while still drawing clear attention to the focal card. Filtering to a subset (the original v1 approach) collapsed the tree too aggressively and removed useful peripheral context.
alternativesConsidered: Filter visibleTree to focused + ancestors + children only (original implementation) — removed spatial context, felt like a mode switch rather than a zoom

decisions.decision: Breadcrumb trail as the primary 'hop up' affordance
decisions.rationale: A breadcrumb renders the full ancestor path (e.g. feat-001 › feat-005 › feat-014) and lets readers click any ancestor to re-focus on that level. One click to go up one level, or skip directly to the root.
alternativesConsidered: Back button (browser history style) — only goes to the last visited node, not the parent in the tree
Up arrow button — suggests a single step, doesn't show the full path

decisions.decision: Focus state lives in URL (query param ?focus=feat-2026-014)
decisions.rationale: Unlike collapse state (ephemeral), focus is a meaningful reading position worth sharing. A URL-encoded focus lets readers bookmark or share the exact slice they care about.
alternativesConsidered: Component state only — not shareable, resets on reload
localStorage — persists but not shareable, can confuse returning visitors

decisions.decision: history.replaceState instead of pushState for URL sync
decisions.rationale: Focus changes are navigation within the same view, not new history entries. replaceState keeps the browser back button pointing to the referring page rather than building a stack of focus states.
alternativesConsidered: pushState — adds back-button navigation through focus history, useful but complex for v1

implementation: Focus mode is implemented as a URL query param (?focus=<featureKey>) read in App.tsx via URLSearchParams, synced back with history.replaceState on every change. When a focusKey is set, the visibleTree is NOT filtered — all non-collapsed cards remain in the DOM. Instead, a focusContextKeys Set is computed (ancestors walked via lineage.parent + direct children from childrenMap), and each card receives a focusState prop: 'focused' for the target card, 'context' for ancestors/children, 'dimmed' for everything else. CSS classes on the feature-entry wrapper handle the visual differentiation (see feat-2026-016). The breadcrumb component walks the ancestor chain from the focused feature to the root and renders a clickable path — each crumb calls setFocusKey. 'Hop up' is the second-to-last crumb. An '← all' button clears focusKey and restores normal mode.
knownLimitations: URL-encoded focus requires the app to parse query params — currently the app has no router, so this needs a lightweight URL sync layer (e.g. URLSearchParams in useEffect)
Breadcrumb can get long for deeply nested features with verbose keys — no truncation strategy defined yet
Focus mode with ?focus= in URL could confuse visitors who land on a shared link without knowing the full tree exists
Collapse-all scoped to focused sub-tree requires tracking which features are in scope — adds complexity to the collapse state shape
successCriteria: A reader can click 'focus' on any feature card, see only that feature + its ancestors + direct children, navigate up via breadcrumb, collapse all children with one click, and share the URL to give someone else the same focused view. Clearing focus restores the full tree.

lineage.spawnReason: Collapse/expand (feat-2026-005) handles dismissal; this feature handles intentional navigation — repositioning to the story that matters rather than hiding the stories that don't.
